column3: Step / Function
column4: Expected Result

column3: Open the Marvin documentation site (this site) in a browser.
column4: The docs load: Home, Creating Tests, Example Tests, and the install/UAT pages are all reachable.

column3: Open Creating Tests and scroll to the driver API and the AI prompt.
column4: The page shows the web and desktop driver methods and a copy-pasteable AI prompt.

column3: Launch Marvin.
column4: A splash appears, then a diagnostics screen before the main dashboard.

column3: Review the diagnostics checks.
column4: Node, OS, Git, Chrome, ChromeDriver, PowerShell, and Scheduler each show a pass/warn/fail badge.

column3: Click Continue.
column4: The main dashboard opens, with the Marvin title and logo at the top.

column3: Click Web Tests, then Desktop Tests.
column4: The mode switches; test cards show web options (OKTA, Visual Browser) in Web mode and hide them in Desktop mode.

column3: Switch mode with a non-empty run sequence.
column4: You are prompted to confirm, and the sequence clears on confirm.

column3: Restart Marvin.
column4: The last selected mode is remembered.

column3: Enter a name in Tester name (for Zephyr reporting).
column4: The value is accepted and persists across restarts.

column3: Enter your ID in Atlassian account ID (for Zephyr reporting).
column4: The value is accepted and persists across restarts.

column3: Click Open Secrets.
column4: The Secrets panel opens showing the default secrets (ZEPHYR_API_TOKEN, GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN, GITHUB_USERNAME) with blank/masked values.

column3: Add a new secret (name + value), click Save.
column4: It is added and its value is masked, never shown in plain text.

column3: Edit an existing secret's value and save.
column4: The new value is stored; the field stays masked.

column3: Delete a secret.
column4: You are asked to confirm, and it is removed.

column3: Set ZEPHYR_API_TOKEN, GITHUB_USERNAME, and GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN to real values.
column4: Saved, for the repo and Zephyr steps below.

column3: Paste a public repo URL and click Refresh Tests.
column4: The tests under the repo's tests/ folder appear as test cards.

column3: Click the private repo help (PAT) button.
column4: A popup explains how to create a GitHub token, with the steps image.

column3: Tick private repo, set GITHUB_USERNAME and GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN as secrets, paste a private repo URL, and Refresh Tests.
column4: The private repo clones and its tests appear.

column3: Refresh Tests again while a test folder is in use.
column4: It refreshes without an EBUSY error.

column3: Open Creating Tests in the docs and copy the AI prompt.
column4: The prompt copies cleanly.

column3: Paste it into your AI assistant with a description of a test you want, and get back a run.js.
column4: The AI returns a run.js following the documented format.

column3: Put it in your test repo as tests/<name>/run.js, push, then Refresh Tests in Marvin.
column4: The new test appears as a card and can be added to a sequence.

column3: Add a test to the run sequence.
column4: It appears in the Run Sequence sidebar.

column3: Fill in any parameters on the card.
column4: Values are retained.

column3: Reference a secret in a parameter (${{ secrets.NAME }}) that does not exist.
column4: The card shows a warning badge for the undefined secret.

column3: Enter Zephyr Project/Case/Cycle keys on the card.
column4: Accepted; malformed keys are flagged on dry run (below).

column3: In Web mode, set an OKTA environment and toggle Visual Browser.
column4: Both options are available on web test cards.

column3: With a configured sequence, click Dry Run (validate only).
column4: A report shows PASS/WARN/FAIL per test: empty required parameters, missing secret references, and bad Zephyr key formats are flagged. No test actually runs.

column3: In Desktop mode, add a built-in test (for example the Notepad showcase) and click Run Sequence.
column4: The test runs, live log output streams into the panel, and it finishes with a pass/fail summary.

column3: In Web mode, add a web test and Run Sequence.
column4: Chrome launches (visible if Visual Browser is on), the test runs, results stream in.

column3: Run a sequence of two or more tests.
column4: They run in order and each reports its own result.

column3: Start a longer run, then click the red Stop button.
column4: The run halts within a couple of seconds and the log ends with === Stopped by user ===.

column3: After stopping, start another run.
column4: The UI is back to idle and a new run starts normally.

column3: Click Show Server-side Test Logs.
column4: The server-side run log is shown.

column3: Click the Marvin logs button.
column4: Marvin's own (app) log output is shown.

column3: Run a test that fails on screen.
column4: A failure screenshot is captured and shown inline in the log.

column3: Run a test with valid Zephyr keys and a set ZEPHYR_API_TOKEN.
column4: A result is posted to the Zephyr Scale test cycle.

column3: Set only Tester name (no Atlassian ID) and run.
column4: The execution comment in Zephyr includes Executed by: <name>.

column3: Set a valid Atlassian account ID and run.
column4: Zephyr's native Executed by and Assigned to fields are populated from the account ID.

column3: Click + New Schedule, set a name, time, and days from the current sequence, and save.
column4: The schedule appears in the Scheduled Sequences list.

column3: Click Run Now on a schedule.
column4: It executes immediately, the same as a manual run.

column3: Pause then Resume a schedule.
column4: Its status changes accordingly and it stops/starts firing on schedule.

column3: Edit a schedule (name, time, days, notifications, Zephyr identity).
column4: Changes are saved.

column3: Toggle a schedule's Logs.
column4: The last run's log is shown.

column3: Delete a schedule.
column4: You confirm, and it is removed.

column3: Confirm a schedule created by one user is visible to another user on the same machine.
column4: Schedules are shared across users.

column3: On a schedule, set an ntfy topic (and optional server) and let it run.
column4: An ntfy notification with the run result is delivered.

column3: Set a Teams webhook (all results) and a Teams webhook (failures only).
column4: Every run posts to the all-results webhook; a failing run also posts to the failures webhook, including logs.

column3: Click Export on a schedule, set a password, and save the .utsb file.
column4: An encrypted .utsb bundle is downloaded.

column3: Click Import Schedule, choose the .utsb, and enter the password.
column4: The schedule, its test code, images, and secrets are restored and it appears in the list.

column3: Try importing with the wrong password.
column4: Import fails cleanly with an error, not a crash.
